text: Organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 (OATP1B1) is a multispecific hepatic transporter that takes up a large number of endogenous substrates and clinical drugs including bile salts, bilirubin and statins. Despite its key role in drug pharmacokinetics and drug–drug interactions (DDIs), the structural reason for its polyspecificity and how it functions during transport is poorly understood. Here, we report cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 in its outward- and inward-open conformations in both its apo form as well as with its representative ligands such as the endogenous compounds bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate as well as the antiviral drug simeprevir and the fluorescent dye 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein. Together, these show that the functional translocation path contains a conserved orthosteric aromatic binding hub along with additional substrate-binding major and minor pockets. Substrate translocation occurs through a rocker-switch mechanism, which involves substantial changes in the TMDs (transmembrane domains). Through the integration of these structures with a combination of molecular dynamics simulations and point mutants, the structural basis for the polyspecificity of OATP1B1 is fully explained and serves as a paradigm for understanding the structure and function of members of the OATP family as well as in the prediction of DDI and improved drug delivery to the liver.
